text.title: Stakeholder Management Anti-Patterns and Agent Gotchas
text.summary: Concrete failure modes PMs face when managing stakeholders, including PM-as-bottleneck, upward-comms inflation, lateral capture, and LLM-specific risks.

antipattern.description: PM centralizes all cross-functional communication through themselves (PM as bottleneck).
antipattern.reason: The register should route, not funnel — some engineering-to-sales communication should happen directly with the PM informed, not as a relay. Single-point-of-failure PMs create delays and knowledge silos.

antipattern.description: PM escalates every decision upward, training executives to ignore communications and the team to bypass the PM.
antipattern.reason: Define explicitly what does NOT go upward as deliberately as what does; keep a written "I will decide" column on the decision log.

antipattern.description: Lateral capture: a friendly head-of-sales becomes the loudest voice and silently steers the roadmap while appearing as one row among many in the register.
antipattern.reason: Track time spent per stakeholder monthly; if it does not match power x interest, recalibrate. The grid becomes inaccurate when attention diverges from the model.

antipattern.description: Same communication format and content sent to all stakeholders regardless of role.
antipattern.reason: Executives need outcomes and risk; engineers need decisions and constraints; customers need benefits and how-to. One message serves none of them well.

antipattern.description: Ignoring silent stakeholders — not proactively seeking input from those who don't speak up in meetings.
antipattern.reason: Silent stakeholders who were not engaged become the loudest blockers at launch; they had concerns they never voiced because nobody asked.

antipattern.description: Only sharing positive updates; hiding challenges until they are crises.
antipattern.reason: Executive trust is built by surfacing bad news early, not by delivering only good news — which trains executives to assume the PM is hiding problems.

antipattern.description: No escalation path defined — no explicit process for resolving conflicts within a time limit.
antipattern.reason: Without an escalation path, conflicts fester; an explicit "unresolved in 48h escalates to executive sponsor" creates the pressure needed to resolve things before they block delivery.

section.title: Agent-Specific Gotchas

rule.statement: Require the agent to cite a policy document, RACI matrix, or written email when populating decision_rights; lint for unsourced rows.
rule.rationale: Hallucinated decision rights are the most damaging LLM error in stakeholder management — an agent confidently asserting "Head of Legal must approve" when a delegate does causes process delays and legal confusion.

rule.statement: Force executive briefs to lead with the worst news in section one, reject drafts that bury risk in section three.
rule.rationale: LLMs default to softening bad news for high-power readers; structural rules override sycophancy where stakes are highest.

rule.statement: All escalations must go through humans — never automate an escalation that emails an executive's manager based on sentiment analysis.
rule.rationale: Auto-escalation based on inferred executive sentiment is a fireable offense; the consequence is irreversible and the input (tone-scoring) is unreliable.

section.reference: Script flagging PM time vs. stakeholder power-x-interest mismatch
Communication matrix and escalation path template
